headline: The Tatis Era Begins, Trout Gets His Discount, and Someone Actually Drafted Greg Holland in 2021
subheadline: A 15-round odyssey through the most chaotic, star-studded, and occasionally baffling draft in MillerLite BeerLeagueBaseball history.
body:
**I. The War Room Opens**

There's a scene in *Moneyball* where Billy Beane is pacing the hallways during the draft, too anxious to sit still, crushing cans of soda like they personally insulted his mother. That's how I imagine all 14 managers in MillerLite BeerLeagueBaseball on March 22, 2021 — except instead of soda, it's actual Miller Lites, and instead of billion-dollar franchises, we're fighting over whether **Keston Hiura** is a bounce-back candidate or a strikeout machine who happens to own a baseball glove. (Spoiler: probably the latter, but we'll get there.)

This draft had everything. We had **Fernando Tatis Jr.** going first overall like he's LeBron in 2003. We had someone taking **Greg Holland** in the eighth round like it's still 2017 and we're all wearing fidget spinners. We had back-to-back ace selections that felt like the pitching equivalent of the Malice at the Palace — just absolute chaos with everyone throwing haymakers. The steals were real (shoutout to whoever got **Julio Urías** in the ninth round), and the reaches were reacher than when your uncle tries to explain cryptocurrency at Thanksgiving dinner.

But here's the thing about snake drafts in a 14-team league: the middle matters more than the ends. Sure, picking first means you get Tatis, but it also means you're waiting 23 picks for your next selection. That turn at picks 13-14 and 15-16? That's where championships are forged. That's where you find out who actually did their homework and who's still relying on 2019 rankings they printed at the office. Let's dive in.

**II. Draft Order & Early-Pick Breakdown**

The top of this draft was absolutely loaded, and for the most part, managers didn't screw it up — which, if you've been in fantasy leagues long enough, you know is basically a miracle. **Zach's Team** had the unenviable task of making the first overall selection, and they went with **Fernando Tatis Jr.**, who slashed .277/.366/.571 with 17 homers and 11 stolen bases in the shortened 2020 campaign. That's a 162-game pace of roughly 45 HR and 30 SB. In a categories league with steals? That's your guy. No notes.

**Pee Wee Reetz** grabbed **Mookie Betts** at #2, which — fine, sure, Mookie hit .292 with an .867 OPS and won a ring, but are we maybe overthinking the name brand here? **HVR Hits Different** snagged **Ronald Acuña Jr.** at #3, and that's a slam dunk — the man posted a .987 OPS in 2020 with elite speed. **Brian Norton** took **Juan Soto** at #4, who literally walked more than he struck out like some kind of baseball wizard. The first four picks were essentially a murderer's row of "you can't mess this up" selections. And then we got to pick #5, where **AbbotsfordColbyTwins** took **Mike Trout**, and suddenly we're back to debating whether the best player in baseball should've gone #1 overall. (He should have. He always should. But I get it — age, injuries, the Angels being the Angels. It's complicated.)

**III. The Draft Grades: All 14 Teams**

**Zach's Team (Pick #1)**

Look, when you pick first overall and you land **Fernando Tatis Jr.**, you've already won the first round. But this draft was a masterclass in roster construction after that. **Anthony Rendon** at pick 24 is solid if unspectacular (he hit .286 with 9 HR in the shortened season, so we're banking on health). **Jack Flaherty** at 25 gives you an ace-level arm (4.91 ERA in 2020 was rough, but the 2.75 ERA in 2019 is the real Flaherty). The **Kevin Gausman** pick at 96 is where this draft really shines — he posted a 3.62 ERA with a 2.81 FIP in 2020, which screams breakout. **Giancarlo Stanton** at 97 is the ultimate boom-or-bust play; he mashed .250/.387/.500 when healthy, which is roughly 40% of the time. The late-round pitching depth with **Pablo López** and **Elieser Hernández** shows a manager who understands roster building. **Grade: A-** (The Tatis pick carries a lot of weight, and the mid-round pitching is sneaky good.)

**Pee Wee Reetz's Team (Pick #2)**

The **Mookie Betts** selection at #2 is defensible, even if I think Acuña or Soto were better values. But here's where things get spicy: **Clayton Kershaw** at pick 23 in a 12-category league that values quality starts? That's a bit early for a guy who threw only 58.1 innings in 2020 with a 2.16 ERA but has legitimate durability concerns. **Alex Bregman** at 26 is interesting — he slashed .242/.350/.451 in 2020, which is fine but not third-round elite. **Aaron Judge** at 47 is the annual "will he play 120 games?" lottery ticket. The **Sandy Alcantara** pick at 143 is excellent value for a guy with a 3.00 ERA and workhorse potential. But taking **Leody Taveras** at pick 119? He hit .227 with a .609 OPS as a rookie. That's a reach that hurts. **Grade: B** (Solid foundation, but the Taveras pick and early Kershaw selection raise eyebrows.)

**HVR Hits Different (Pick #3)**

This team name slaps, and so does this draft. **Ronald Acuña Jr.** at #3 is perfect — the .250/.406/.581 slash with 14 HR and 8 SB in 60 games is absurd. **Adalberto Mondesi** at 22 is the ultimate high-risk, high-reward play; he's basically the stolen base cheat code when healthy (24 SB in 59 games in 2020), but "when healthy" is doing a lot of heavy lifting. **Brandon Woodruff** at 27 is a steal — 3.05 ERA, 0.99 WHIP, elite strikeout stuff. **Kyle Tucker** at 46 is the pick that separates the smart managers from the sheep; he posted a .512 slugging percentage and looks like a star. **Josh Hader** at 51 is fine for saves and holds, and **Yordan Alvarez** at 70 is a bet on the knee holding up after he missed all of 2020. The **Salvador Perez** pick at 118 is value city — catcher is a wasteland, and Salvy provides pop. **Grade: A** (Elite top-five picks, smart mid-round targets, and enough upside to win the whole thing.)

**Brian Norton's Team (Pick #4)**

The **Juan Soto** pick at #4 is chef's kiss. The man posted a 1.185 OPS in 2020 at age 21. He's not human. **Lucas Giolito** at 21 gives you a legit ace (3.48 ERA, 97 Ks in 72.2 IP), and **Francisco Lindor** at 28 is the kind of safe floor play you want in round three. **Rafael Devers** at 45 is interesting — he hit .263/.310/.483 with 11 HR, so the power is real but the OBP in a league that counts it is a concern. The deep sleeper here is **Luis Robert Jr.** at pick 52, who was the AL ROY runner-up and looks like a five-tool monster (11 HR, 9 SB in 56 games). **Dustin May** at 172 is an excellent late-round flier on an elite arm. **Grade: A-** (Soto anchors everything, and the Robert pick could define the season.)

**AbbotsfordColbyTwins (Pick #5)**

**Mike Trout** at #5 is Mike Trout. You know what you're getting: the best player in baseball who hit .281/.390/.603 with 17 HR in 53 games. **Walker Buehler** at 20 is an elite SP2 (1.89 FIP in 2020, absolutely filthy). Here's where it gets dicey: **Marcell Ozuna** at 29 is riding high off a monster .338/.431/.636 season that screams regression. **Vladimir Guerrero Jr.** at 44 is a bet on the breakout finally happening (he hit .262 with a .791 OPS in 2020, which is... fine?). **Keston Hiura** at 68 is a scary pick — he struck out 85 times in 59 games with a .212 average. That's a lot of trust for a guy who might have a hole in his swing the size of Wisconsin. The **Marcus Semien** pick at 140 is sneaky good value. **Grade: B** (Trout and Buehler are excellent, but the Ozuna regression risk and Hiura question marks loom large.)

**Benchwarmers (Pick #6)**

Oh boy. Let's talk about **Christian Yelich** at #6. He hit .205/.356/.430 in 2020. That's... not great, Bob! (Mad Men reference, stay with me.) Now, is he going to bounce back? Probably. But you're banking on 2018-2019 Yelich, not 2020 Yelich, and that's a risk at the sixth overall pick. **Max Scherzer** at 19 is vintage Scherzer (3.74 ERA, but he's 36 and the innings are piling up). **Aaron Nola** at 30 is fine (3.28 ERA, elite Ks). But then we get to **Cavan Biggio** at 43. He hit .250 with 8 HR in 59 games. That's a fourth-round pick on a guy with a 104 wRC+. **Devin Williams** at 54 is incredible value for a reliever who posted a 0.33 ERA and won Rookie of the Year. **Greg Holland** at 91 is... I'm sorry, what year is it? Holland had a 1.91 ERA in 28 innings, but he's 34 and the saves aren't guaranteed. This feels like a 2017 draft board that got accidentally uploaded. **Grade: C+** (The Yelich gamble and some head-scratching mid-round picks drag this down.)

**Marcus' P.B. Fort (Pick #7)**

Going pitcher at #7 overall with **Jacob deGrom** is a bold strategy, Cotton. Let's see if it pays off. (deGrom's 2020: 2.38 ERA, 0.96 WHIP, 104 Ks in 68 IP. So yes, it probably pays off.) **Bryce Harper** at 18 is solid (.962 OPS in 2020), and **Xander Bogaerts** at 31 gives you a premium bat at a premium position (.300/.364/.502). **Whit Merrifield** at 42 is the definition of a safe floor — he'll give you .282, 9 HR, and 12 SB. **Liam Hendriks** at 55 is elite closer value (1.78 ERA, 14.1 K/9). The **Teoscar Hernández** pick at 127 is a steal — he hit .289 with 16 HR in 50 games and nobody's talking about him. **Grade: B+** (deGrom is a gamble at 7, but the mid-round value is strong.)

**BaseballPro77 (Pick #8)**

Another pitcher in the top 10! **Gerrit Cole** at #8 is defensible — he went 7-3 with a 2.84 ERA in 2020 and is the definition of a safe ace. **Manny Machado** at 17 gives you the .304/.370/.580 slash from his San Diego bounce-back. **Bo Bichette** at 32 is the upside play of the draft; he hit .301 in his limited 2020 action. **Luis Castillo** at 41 is the buy-low pick of the century — his 3.21 ERA doesn't tell the whole story of his elite stuff. **Kris Bryant** at 128 is a nice late-round flier on a former MVP. The **Patrick Corbin** pick at 152 is questionable (4.66 ERA in 2020), but at that point you're just throwing darts. **Grade: B+** (Cole is safe money, and the Machado-Bichette-Castillo run is excellent.)

**Music City Trash Can (Pick #9)**

Love the team name. Love the shade. **Trea Turner** at #9 is exactly where he should go — .335 average, 12 HR, 12 SB in 59 games. That's a first-round lock. **Yu Darvish** at 16 is coming off a Cy Young-caliber season (2.01 ERA, 0.96 WHIP). **José Abreu** at 33 just won AL MVP with a .317/.370/.617 line and 19 HR in 60 games. **Ozzie Albies** at 40 provides elite multi-position eligibility with a .271 average. **Randy Arozarena** at 57 is the poster child for "what did he just do in the playoffs?" — the man hit .377 with 10 HR in the postseason. The **Joe Musgrove** pick at 88 is interesting (3.86 ERA in 2020, but flashes of brilliance). **Grade: A-** (Turner-Darvish-Abreu is an elite opening trio, and Arozarena has league-winner upside.)

**This is Shira Lazar (Pick #10)**

The boldest strategy of the draft: **Shane Bieber** at #10, followed immediately by **Trevor Bauer** at #15. That's the 2020 AL Cy Young winner (1.63 ERA, 122 Ks in 77.1 IP) and the NL Cy Young winner (1.73 ERA, 100 Ks in 73 IP) on the same team. In a 12-category league that values IP, K, ERA, WHIP, and QS, this is either genius or insanity. (It's probably genius.) **Corey Seager** at 34 gives you the World Series MVP (.307/.358/.585), and **Nolan Arenado** at 39 is a bet on the Rockies-to-Cardinals trade rejuvenating his career. **Lance Lynn** at 58 is sneaky elite (3.32 ERA, underrated workhorse). The **Byron Buxton** pick at 159 is the annual "will Buxton finally stay healthy?" lottery ticket. **Grade: A** (The Bieber-Bauer stack is unprecedented and potentially league-winning.)

**Albuquerque Bombers (Pick #11)**

**Freddie Freeman** at #11 is the safest pick in the draft. The man hit .341/.462/.640 with 13 HR in 60 games and won NL MVP. That's your guy. **Trevor Story** at 14 gives you 11 HR and 15 SB in 59 games from the shortstop position. **Blake Snell** at 35 is a former Cy Young winner whose 3.24 ERA in 2020 undersells his stuff. **Corbin Burnes** at 38 is the pick of the draft — he posted a 2.11 ERA with a 0.84 WHIP in 2020 and looks like a future ace. **Jesse Winker** at 134 is excellent value for a guy who hit .255 with a .394 OBP. The **Jarred Kelenic** pick at 179 is the ultimate dynasty stash if this were a keeper league. **Grade: A** (Freeman anchors, Burnes is a steal, and the Story-Snell combo is dangerous.)

**Mel's Purple Nurples (Pick #12)**

Picking 12th in a 14-team snake draft is actually advantageous — you get back-to-back selections at 12/13 and 36/37. **Mel** capitalized with **José Ramírez** at 12 (.292/.386/.607 with 17 HR and 10 SB) and **Cody Bellinger** at 13. Here's the problem: Bellinger hit .239/.333/.455 in 2020, which is fine but not back-to-back-first-round pick fine. **Tyler Glasnow** at 36 is an elite arm (4.08 ERA but 14.3 K/9), and **DJ LeMahieu** at 37 provides the best contact bat in baseball (.364 average in 2020). **Stephen Strasburg** at 61 is a risk — he threw only 5 innings in 2020 before injury. **Julio Urías** at 108 is highway robbery (3.27 ERA, Dodgers trust him). **Will Smith (catcher)** at 131 is excellent positional value. **Grade: B+** (Ramírez and LeMahieu are perfect, but Bellinger's struggles and Strasburg's health are concerns.)

**IV. Draft Day Heroes & Villains**

**The Three Best Value Picks:**

1. **Corbin Burnes, Round 4, Pick 38 (Albuquerque Bombers)** — A guy who posted a 2.11 ERA with an 0.84 WHIP falling to pick 38 is criminal. Burnes has ace upside and was drafted like a back-end starter. This could be the pick that wins the league.

2. **Julio Urías, Round 9, Pick 108 (Mel's Purple Nurples)** — The Dodgers' playoff starter, a guy with a 3.27 ERA and clear path to 180+ innings, going in the ninth round? That's free money.

3. **Teoscar Hernández, Round 11, Pick 127 (Marcus' P.B. Fort)** — He hit .289 with 16 HR in 50 games and was treated like an afterthought. In a league that counts batting average, this is a steal.

**The Three Worst Reaches:**

1. **Greg Holland, Round 8, Pick 91 (Benchwarmers)** — I genuinely thought my spreadsheet was broken when I saw this. Greg Holland. In the eighth round. In 2021. He's 34, his fastball is gone, and the saves aren't guaranteed. This is a 15th-round pick at best.

2. **Leody Taveras, Round 10, Pick 119 (Pee Wee Reetz's Team)** — A .227 hitter with a .609 OPS going ahead of established veterans is tough to justify. The upside is there, but this is a deep-league pick in a 14-team league where better options existed.

3. **Cavan Biggio, Round 4, Pick 43 (Benchwarmers)** — A 104 wRC+ hitter going in the fourth round is a reach, even with the positional flexibility. Biggio is fine, but fourth-round fine? That's aggressive.

**V. Five Bold Predictions for 2021**

1. **This is Shira Lazar's Bieber-Bauer Stack Leads the League in Pitching Categories** — Having both Cy Young winners on one team is going to result in dominant pitching numbers. They'll lead the league in Ks and QS by Week 8.

2. **Brian Norton's Luis Robert Jr. Finishes as a Top-10 Overall Player** — The 23-year-old's 2020 was just the appetizer. He's going to hit 30 HR with 20 SB and be a league-winner.

3. **Benchwarmers Finishes in the Bottom Three Despite Having Yelich and Scherzer** — That Greg Holland pick is symbolic of a draft that never quite found its footing. Too many questionable reaches in the middle rounds.

4. **HVR Hits Different's Adalberto Mondesi Plays Fewer Than 100 Games** — It happens every year. Mondesi's body betrays his elite tools. The 24-SB-in-59-games ceiling becomes a 40-game injury nightmare.

5. **Albuquerque Bombers' Corbin Burnes Finishes With a Sub-3.00 ERA and Top-5 SP Value** — The pick 38 steal becomes the defining pick of the season. Burnes emerges as a bonafide ace.

**VI. The Pick: Champion Crowned**

If you put a gun to my head and told me to pick the 2021 MillerLite BeerLeagueBaseball champion, I'm going with **HVR Hits Different**.

Here's why: In a 12-category league, you need elite contributors in both hitting and pitching categories. **Ronald Acuña Jr.** is a five-category monster who can single-handedly win you HR, R, SB, and AVG in any given week. **Brandon Woodruff** and **Zack Wheeler** give them a pitching core that can compete with anyone. **Kyle Tucker** is a breakout waiting to happen. **Salvador Perez** provides actual production from the catcher position, which is basically cheating. And if **Yordan Alvarez** comes back healthy? Forget about it.

The **Mondesi** pick is the swing factor — if he plays 130 games, this team wins by 40 points. If he plays 80, they're still in the mix. The roster construction is balanced, the upside is immense, and the draft slot (#3) allowed them to secure Acuña while still getting elite value at the turns.

**This is Shira Lazar** is my dark horse with that Bieber-Bauer stack, and **Albuquerque Bombers** has the safest floor with Freeman and Burnes. But give me the team with the best player in the draft (Acuña), the best positional value (Perez), and the highest ceiling (Mondesi when healthy).

HVR Hits Different isn't just a team name. It's a prediction.

*And yes, I'm still thinking about that Greg Holland pick. I'll be thinking about it all season. Some things you just can't unsee.*
